ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novel contribution by proposing a new automatic metric (RAINBOW) that reportedly achieves much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments, and by being the first study to target end-to-end data-driven NLG that can produce ungrammatical output while comparing three generation approaches. Its large-scale comparison of 21 automated metrics (including novel grammar-based metrics) across three datasets and three systems clearly differentiates it from prior work. These elements go beyond a straightforward replication or minor tweak, though they are primarily empirical and evaluative rather than introducing a wholly new theoretical framework. | B(4): The paper introduces a clearly new metric (RAINBOW) and an unusually broad, focused evaluation comparing 21 automated metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) across three datasets and three end-to-end data-driven NLG systems — claimed as the first study to target ungrammatical outputs in this setting. Its emphasis on sentence-level correlations and detailed error analysis that shows limitations of existing metrics differentiates it from prior work. However, the general problem of automatic NLG evaluation is well-studied, so the contribution is notable and non-trivial but not a radical departure from existing research.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novel contribution by proposing a new automatic metric (RAINBOW) that reportedly achieves much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments, and by being the first study to target end-to-end data-driven NLG that can produce ungrammatical output while comparing three generation approaches. Its large-scale comparison of 21 automated metrics (including novel grammar-based metrics) across three datasets and three systems clearly differentiates it from prior work. These elements go beyond a straightforward replication or minor tweak, though they are primarily empirical and evaluative rather than introducing a wholly new theoretical framework.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new metric (RAINBOW) and an unusually broad, focused evaluation comparing 21 automated metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) across three datasets and three end-to-end data-driven NLG systems — claimed as the first study to target ungrammatical outputs in this setting. Its emphasis on sentence-level correlations and detailed error analysis that shows limitations of existing metrics differentiates it from prior work. However, the general problem of automatic NLG evaluation is well-studied, so the contribution is notable and non-trivial but not a radical departure from existing research.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses automatic evaluation of data-driven NLG, a core NLP topic and appropriate for top-tier NLP venues. It targets the right audience by comparing 21 metrics (including a proposed RAINBOW metric) against human judgments across three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS). The problem formulation and methodology (large metric set, sentence- and system-level correlations, human Likert ratings) match the community’s evaluation standards, and the paper is well positioned with thorough related work coverage and available code. | B(5): This paper directly addresses core NLP research on automatic evaluation of data-driven NLG, proposing a new metric (RAINBOW) and reporting stronger sentence-level correlation with human judgments — a topic squarely within top NLP/ML venues. The study includes a comprehensive empirical comparison of 21 metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), uses standard human evaluation dimensions, and provides code, matching the methodological and reproducibility expectations of the community. Its findings about metric limitations and dataset/system specificity are highly relevant to the venue’s audience and research directions.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses automatic evaluation of data-driven NLG, a core NLP topic and appropriate for top-tier NLP venues. It targets the right audience by comparing 21 metrics (including a proposed RAINBOW metric) against human judgments across three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS). The problem formulation and methodology (large metric set, sentence- and system-level correlations, human Likert ratings) match the community’s evaluation standards, and the paper is well positioned with thorough related work coverage and available code.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper directly addresses core NLP research on automatic evaluation of data-driven NLG, proposing a new metric (RAINBOW) and reporting stronger sentence-level correlation with human judgments — a topic squarely within top NLP/ML venues. The study includes a comprehensive empirical comparison of 21 metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), uses standard human evaluation dimensions, and provides code, matching the methodological and reproducibility expectations of the community. Its findings about metric limitations and dataset/system specificity are highly relevant to the venue’s audience and research directions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and experimentally thorough—introducing the RAINBOW metric early, comparing 21 metrics across three datasets and three systems, and presenting a detailed error analysis. Main contributions are clearly stated (first end-to-end comparison, new metric, sentence-vs-system-level findings). The summary does not mention notation clarity or hyperparameter reporting, so consistency/definitions there are unclear and represent a minor presentation gap. Figures/tables and examples seem effective given the cross-dataset results reporting, and overall writing quality is described as good. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro -> methods -> experiments -> conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (first end-to-end NLG comparison, 21 metrics, and the RAINBOW metric). Notation and presentation of evaluation metrics are consistent and the extensive figures/tables and detailed error analysis across three datasets make results informative. Minor presentation issues include omission of reported hyperparameters and a need to clarify that the Stanford parser score is not designed to measure grammaticality, but overall the writing quality is good and the flow is logical.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized and experimentally thorough—introducing the RAINBOW metric early, comparing 21 metrics across three datasets and three systems, and presenting a detailed error analysis. Main contributions are clearly stated (first end-to-end comparison, new metric, sentence-vs-system-level findings). The summary does not mention notation clarity or hyperparameter reporting, so consistency/definitions there are unclear and represent a minor presentation gap. Figures/tables and examples seem effective given the cross-dataset results reporting, and overall writing quality is described as good.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro -> methods -> experiments -> conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (first end-to-end NLG comparison, 21 metrics, and the RAINBOW metric). Notation and presentation of evaluation metrics are consistent and the extensive figures/tables and detailed error analysis across three datasets make results informative. Minor presentation issues include omission of reported hyperparameters and a need to clarify that the Stanford parser score is not designed to measure grammaticality, but overall the writing quality is good and the flow is logical.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broad and substantive—21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based ones), three generation systems, and three datasets—supporting the main claims and including a new metric (RAINBOW) and detailed error analysis. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, and hyperparameters are not provided (though code is available), limiting full reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and failure cases and limitations (sentence-level unreliability, poor discrimination of average/good outputs, parser limitations) are explicitly discussed. These are minor gaps against an otherwise solid and comprehensive evaluation. | B(4): Experiments are broad and detailed: 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) are evaluated across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets/domains (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), and the paper proposes and evaluates a new combined metric (RAINBOW) with sentence-level correlations and a detailed error analysis. The authors also discuss limitations and make code available, and related work coverage is reported as thorough. However, the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses for the proposed metric or combinations, and hyperparameters are not reported, which are gaps for full reproducibility and robustness testing. Overall the work is solid and comprehensive with some minor experimental/detailing gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are broad and substantive—21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based ones), three generation systems, and three datasets—supporting the main claims and including a new metric (RAINBOW) and detailed error analysis. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, and hyperparameters are not provided (though code is available), limiting full reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and failure cases and limitations (sentence-level unreliability, poor discrimination of average/good outputs, parser limitations) are explicitly discussed. These are minor gaps against an otherwise solid and comprehensive evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments are broad and detailed: 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) are evaluated across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets/domains (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), and the paper proposes and evaluates a new combined metric (RAINBOW) with sentence-level correlations and a detailed error analysis. The authors also discuss limitations and make code available, and related work coverage is reported as thorough. However, the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses for the proposed metric or combinations, and hyperparameters are not reported, which are gaps for full reproducibility and robustness testing. Overall the work is solid and comprehensive with some minor experimental/detailing gaps.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents extensive experiments (21 metrics including novel grammar-based ones) across three datasets and three established NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) with human judgments on informativeness, naturalness and quality, which supports the central claims about metric reliability and RAINBOW's superior sentence-level correlation. The methodology is largely sound with appropriate baselines and broad metric coverage, but the summary omits details about statistical testing and hyperparameter reporting (hyperparameters not reported), a minor shortcoming. The conclusion that automatic metrics are reasonable at system-level but unreliable at sentence-level and poor at distinguishing average vs. good outputs is consistent with the described error analysis and cross-dataset results. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly operationalized and supported by experiments: it evaluates 21 metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), and reports that the proposed RAINBOW metric attains higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check. The experimental setup is broadly sound (multiple systems/datasets, wide metric coverage, code available), but the summary notes missing hyperparameter reporting and does not mention statistical significance testing, which are minor methodological gaps. Overall the conclusions about metric reliability and sentence-vs-system-level behavior are justified by the presented analyses, with only modest issues that do not overturn the main findings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents extensive experiments (21 metrics including novel grammar-based ones) across three datasets and three established NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) with human judgments on informativeness, naturalness and quality, which supports the central claims about metric reliability and RAINBOW's superior sentence-level correlation. The methodology is largely sound with appropriate baselines and broad metric coverage, but the summary omits details about statistical testing and hyperparameter reporting (hyperparameters not reported), a minor shortcoming. The conclusion that automatic metrics are reasonable at system-level but unreliable at sentence-level and poor at distinguishing average vs. good outputs is consistent with the described error analysis and cross-dataset results.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly operationalized and supported by experiments: it evaluates 21 metrics (including novel grammar-based ones) across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), and reports that the proposed RAINBOW metric attains higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check. The experimental setup is broadly sound (multiple systems/datasets, wide metric coverage, code available), but the summary notes missing hyperparameter reporting and does not mention statistical significance testing, which are minor methodological gaps. Overall the conclusions about metric reliability and sentence-vs-system-level behavior are justified by the presented analyses, with only modest issues that do not overturn the main findings.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—reliable automatic evaluation for end-to-end data-driven NLG—by systematically comparing three generation approaches (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three datasets and 21 metrics, including novel grammar-based measures. Its finding that most metrics are unreliable at the sentence level and its proposal of RAINBOW, which shows much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments, are likely to change evaluation practices and motivate follow-up work. By making code available and highlighting data- and system-specific limitations, the work opens meaningful research directions (better grammar metrics, sentence-level evaluation) and is likely to be widely used, though its domain/system specificity tempers a fully transformative claim. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical problem—reliable evaluation of end-to-end data-driven NLG—by being the first to directly compare three generation approaches (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three datasets and 21 automatic metrics, including novel grammar-based measures. Its proposal of RAINBOW, which shows much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments, plus the finding that common metrics are unreliable at sentence-level and fail to distinguish average vs. good outputs, is likely to change how researchers evaluate NLG and to prompt development of better metrics. Because the study is empirical, covers multiple domains/datasets, and releases code, the results are likely to be adopted and to open new research directions, though they do not by themselves transform the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—reliable automatic evaluation for end-to-end data-driven NLG—by systematically comparing three generation approaches (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three datasets and 21 metrics, including novel grammar-based measures. Its finding that most metrics are unreliable at the sentence level and its proposal of RAINBOW, which shows much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments, are likely to change evaluation practices and motivate follow-up work. By making code available and highlighting data- and system-specific limitations, the work opens meaningful research directions (better grammar metrics, sentence-level evaluation) and is likely to be widely used, though its domain/system specificity tempers a fully transformative claim.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—reliable evaluation of end-to-end data-driven NLG—by being the first to directly compare three generation approaches (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three datasets and 21 automatic metrics, including novel grammar-based measures. Its proposal of RAINBOW, which shows much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments, plus the finding that common metrics are unreliable at sentence-level and fail to distinguish average vs. good outputs, is likely to change how researchers evaluate NLG and to prompt development of better metrics. Because the study is empirical, covers multiple domains/datasets, and releases code, the results are likely to be adopted and to open new research directions, though they do not by themselves transform the field.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper evaluates three established data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three standard datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and compares a large set of 21 automated metrics (BLEU, METEOR, CIDEr, TER, ROUGE, NIST, LEPOR, grammar-based metrics, etc.), which constitutes a comprehensive set of baselines. Comparisons appear fair—metrics are correlated with human judgments on the same system outputs and datasets—and the authors both report when existing metrics work (reasonable at system-level) and where they fail (unreliable at sentence-level, poor at distinguishing average/good). The proposed RAINBOW metric is directly compared and analyzed against the baselines, and the paper’s related-work coverage is described as thorough. | B(5): The paper evaluates three well-known, relevant NLG baselines (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across the same three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and compares 21 automated metrics plus human judgments, indicating fair, same-data/same-conditions comparisons. It provides detailed error analysis of metric behavior, acknowledges limitations (metrics are data- and system-specific and unreliable at sentence level), and shows when existing metrics perform comparably or poorly. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the authors both propose and quantitatively analyze their new RAINBOW metric against prior approaches, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates three established data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three standard datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and compares a large set of 21 automated metrics (BLEU, METEOR, CIDEr, TER, ROUGE, NIST, LEPOR, grammar-based metrics, etc.), which constitutes a comprehensive set of baselines. Comparisons appear fair—metrics are correlated with human judgments on the same system outputs and datasets—and the authors both report when existing metrics work (reasonable at system-level) and where they fail (unreliable at sentence-level, poor at distinguishing average/good). The proposed RAINBOW metric is directly compared and analyzed against the baselines, and the paper’s related-work coverage is described as thorough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates three well-known, relevant NLG baselines (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across the same three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) and compares 21 automated metrics plus human judgments, indicating fair, same-data/same-conditions comparisons. It provides detailed error analysis of metric behavior, acknowledges limitations (metrics are data- and system-specific and unreliable at sentence level), and shows when existing metrics perform comparably or poorly. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the authors both propose and quantitatively analyze their new RAINBOW metric against prior approaches, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

main_claims: First study to target end-to-end data-driven NLG comparing three different generation approaches that can produce ungrammatical output.
Comparison of a large number (21) of automated metrics including novel grammar-based metrics, and reporting results across two domains and three datasets.
Proposal of a new automatic metric, RAINBOW, which shows much higher correlation with human judgments at the sentence level than existing metrics.
Detailed error analysis showing that automatic metrics can be reasonable at system-level but are unreliable at the sentence-level and perform poorly distinguishing average/good outputs.
_summary.methodology: Evaluate correlations between 21 automatic metrics (word-overlap and grammar-based) and human judgments (informativeness, naturalness, quality on a 6-point Likert scale) for outputs of three data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) on three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), and propose a combined metric (RAINBOW).
limitations: Automatic metrics only weakly reflect human judgments and are unreliable at the sentence-level.
Metrics perform poorly at distinguishing average and good-quality outputs (better at extreme/bad cases).
Metric performance is data- and system-specific.
Stanford parser score is not designed to measure grammaticality and better grammar-scoring functions are needed.
